key_insight: XRDiff uses diffusion models to infer crystal structures directly from powder X-ray diffraction patterns, making structure solution possible even from ambiguous, phase-lost measurements.
engineering_relevance: This offers a practical route to automate crystal structure determination from routine PXRD data, with potential impact on materials discovery, characterization, and high-throughput screening.
method: Train a diffusion model on simulated structure–spectrum pairs.
Condition on PXRD plus either stoichiometry or elemental constituents and unit-cell atom count.
Compare full-spectrum and peak-descriptor encodings for robustness to experimental noise.
evidence_signal: The model was evaluated on composition-held-out polymorph datasets and showed strong structure recovery on simulated benchmarks, while peak-based encoding generalized best to experimental PXRD.
watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and realism of PXRD representations, so simulation-to-experiment gaps and noise/artifact mismatch remain a key deployment risk.
paper_title: XRDiff: Crystal Structure Prediction from Powder X-Ray Diffraction Data Using Diffusion Models
